cot_prefix: Okay, I'm going to start by thinking about this question. A man leaves all his money to his widow's sister, whom he plans to marry next year. In many jurisdictions, a will is a legal document that specifies how a person's assets should be distributed after their death. The key point here is that the will must be made while the person is alive and of sound mind. The man is leaving all his money to his "widow's sister." But he's not currently married; he's planning to marry next year. So, it seems like the man is not yet married to the woman he refers to as his "widow." This is a bit confusing because if he's not married to her yet, she can't be his widow. But the will is referring to "his widow's sister," implying that he is married to her, and she has died (making her his widow). However, the man is planning to marry her next year, so he's not currently married to her.
